locked_facts: Ideal gas equation: PV = nRT (R = 8.314 J/mol·K)
Boyle's law: PV = constant at constant T (isothermal)
Charles's law: V/T = constant at constant P (isobaric)
Gay-Lussac's law: P/T = constant at constant V (isochoric)
Avogadro's law: V ∝ n at constant T and P
STP: T = 273.15 K, P = 1 atm; molar volume = 22.4 L
Real gases deviate from ideal behaviour at high P and low T
truth_statements: The ideal gas equation PV = nRT combines Boyle's, Charles's, and Avogadro's laws into a single relation
At constant temperature, doubling the pressure halves the volume (Boyle's law)
At constant pressure, the volume of a gas is directly proportional to its absolute temperature
An ideal gas has molecules with no intermolecular forces and negligible volume — real gases approximate this at low P and high T
The gas constant R = 8.314 J/mol·K connects the macroscopic quantities P, V, T with the amount of gas

aha_moment.what_to_show: Three graphs side by side: PV vs P (Boyle — horizontal line for ideal gas, curves for real), V vs T (Charles — straight line through origin at 0 K = absolute zero), P vs T (Gay-Lussac — straight line through 0 K). All three laws unified by PV = nRT.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Three gas law graphs with PV = nRT shown as the single equation unifying all three
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students memorise three separate laws — seeing them as projections of one equation PV = nRT unifies their understanding

simulation_states.label: Boyle's law
simulation_states.description: Gas at constant T. Increase P → V decreases. PV product stays constant. Hyperbolic P-V curve.
simulation_states.emphasis: Squeeze the gas (more P) → it shrinks (less V). Product PV stays constant.

simulation_states.label: Charles's law
simulation_states.description: Gas at constant P. Heat it (increase T) → V increases. V vs T is a straight line that extrapolates to V = 0 at T = 0 K.
simulation_states.emphasis: Heat the gas at constant pressure → it expands. The line points to absolute zero.

simulation_states.label: Ideal gas equation
simulation_states.description: All three laws combined into PV = nRT. Given any three of P, V, n, T, calculate the fourth. Real gas deviations shown at high P.
simulation_states.emphasis: One equation to rule them all: PV = nRT

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts P, V, or T — the other variable updates via PV = nRT. Toggle between isothermal, isobaric, and isochoric processes on PV diagram.
simulation_states.emphasis: Change one variable — watch the others adjust to satisfy PV = nRT

common_misconceptions.belief: Temperature in gas laws can be in Celsius
common_misconceptions.correction: Gas laws require ABSOLUTE temperature (Kelvin). Using Celsius gives wrong results because Celsius zero is not absolute zero. Always convert: T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show calculation failing with Celsius (negative V) and working with Kelvin

common_misconceptions.belief: Ideal gas law works for all conditions
common_misconceptions.correction: Real gases deviate significantly at high pressure (molecules forced close together) and low temperature (intermolecular forces matter). The ideal gas law is an approximation that works best at low P and high T.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Compare ideal gas prediction with real gas data — deviation visible at high P
